citations.desc: Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC).
citations.narrative:
An approximately 2-week-old male piglet covered under a research protocol was transported to the facility on January 20,
2026, and examined by a veterinarian on this date with no abnormalities noted in the medical record. This animal was
used in a study-approved behavioral experiment on January 21, 2026. The institution specifies in its “Acclimation of Large
Animals Policy” that a 7-day acclimation period is required before animals are used for any survival experiments.
The animal was provided with food on January 20, 2026, but laboratory personnel requested that the facility delay
morning feeding on January 21, 2026, until after the behavioral experiment was completed. However, fasting prior to
experiments was not an approved activity under the research protocol. The piglet was found by veterinary staff later in the
day shivering and in lateral recumbency, with mild hindlimb ataxia observed after the animal began walking. The animal
was provided with supportive care, including 5% dextrose solution, and monitored closely until it was fully recovered from
this incident.
Additionally, on November 10, 2025, a piglet used in a study-approved MRI on the above-mentioned research protocol
was anesthetized but not intubated as required under the protocol. No adverse effects were noted other than a drop in
oxygen saturation below 94%.
Fasting without prior protocol approval and altering anesthetic procedures constitute significant deviations from the
approved activities in this research protocol, while experimental use of the animals within the acclimation period
constitutes a significant deviation from an institutional policy. Failure to adhere to activities covered under the research
protocols and institutional policies prevents full IACUC oversight of research activities involving animals and has resulted
in severe animal welfare impacts. Principal investigators must follow the IACUC-approved proposals and institutional
policies, and any proposed changes shall be reviewed and approved by the IACUC prior to implementation.
This incident was reported in the IACUC meeting minutes and corrective measures, including increased feeding
frequencies, the use of detailed feed logs, and a full review of the “Acclimation of Large Animals Policy”, had been
implemented prior to the inspection.

citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care.
citations.narrative:
On December 15, 2025, a male rhesus macaque covered under a research protocol was administered Simbadol
(buprenorphine) that was 10 days past the manufacturer’s suggested use of 56 days after the first puncture of the vial, but
still within the manufacturer’s expiration date for an unpunctured vial. There were no adverse effects noted from the
administration of this expired medication.
An expired medication may not be safe or effective and is not an appropriate method for the treatment or prevention of
animal health issues. The facility must ensure that methods of treatment, including the medications maintained for use in
animals, are appropriate.
This incident was reported in the IACUC meeting minutes and corrective measures, including ensuring that vials are
appropriately labeled with expiration dates, had been implemented prior to the inspection.

On January 8, 2026, a male cynomolgus macaque was found to have escaped from its primary enclosure. This animal
sustained a minor hand injury and other minor wounds that were evaluated and treated by a veterinarian, which included
suturing the hand wound as well as administering antibiotics and analgesics. Upon further investigation, the animal was
discovered to have escaped by pushing a pan through a latch and exploiting the resulting gap that would contain a larger
animal.
Failure to house nonhuman primates in secure primary enclosures may allow them to escape and sustain injuries. The
facility must ensure that primary enclosures are constructed and maintained to contain nonhuman primates securely and
prevent accidental opening of the enclosure, including opening by the animal.
This incident was reported in the IACUC meeting minutes and corrective measures, including locking the latch that the
animal exploited, had been implemented prior to the inspection. To remain corrected from April 30, 2026, forward.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
n

narrative:
2.31(c)(7)  Critical Repeat
   
Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC). 
An approximately 2-week-old male piglet covered under a research protocol was transported to the facility on January 20, 
2026, and examined by a veterinarian on this date with no abnormalities noted in the medical record. This animal was 
used in a study-approved behavioral experiment on January 21, 2026. The institution specifies in its “Acclimation of Large 
Animals Policy” that a 7-day acclimation period is required before animals are used for any survival experiments.  
 
The animal was provided with food on January 20, 2026, but laboratory personnel requested that the facility delay 
morning feeding on January 21, 2026, until after the behavioral experiment was completed. However, fasting prior to 
experiments was not an approved activity under the research protocol. The piglet was found by veterinary staff later in the 
day shivering and in lateral recumbency, with mild hindlimb ataxia observed after the animal began walking. The animal 
was provided with supportive care, including 5% dextrose solution, and monitored closely until it was fully recovered from 
this incident. 
 
Additionally, on November 10, 2025, a piglet used in a study-approved MRI on the above-mentioned research protocol 
was anesthetized but not intubated as required under the protocol. No adverse effects were noted other than a drop in 
oxygen saturation below 94%.  
 
Fasting without prior protocol approval and altering anesthetic procedures constitute significant deviations from the 
approved activities in this research protocol, while experimental use of the animals within the acclimation period 
constitutes a significant deviation from an institutional policy. Failure to adhere to activities covered under the research 
protocols and institutional policies prevents full IACUC oversight of research activities involving animals and has resulted 
in severe animal welfare impacts. Principal investigators must follow the IACUC-approved proposals and institutional 
policies, and any proposed changes shall be reviewed and approved by the IACUC prior to implementation. 
 
This incident was reported in the IACUC meeting minutes and corrective measures, including increased feeding 
frequencies, the use of detailed feed logs, and a full review of the “Acclimation of Large Animals Policy”, had been 
implemented prior to the inspection.

2.33(b)(2)  Repeat
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care. 
On December 15, 2025, a male rhesus macaque covered under a research protocol was administered Simbadol 
(buprenorphine) that was 10 days past the manufacturer’s suggested use of 56 days after the first puncture of the vial, but 
still within the manufacturer’s expiration date for an unpunctured vial. There were no adverse effects noted from the 
administration of this expired medication. 
 
An expired medication may not be safe or effective and is not an appropriate method for the treatment or prevention of 
animal health issues. The facility must ensure that methods of treatment, including the medications maintained for use in 
animals, are appropriate. 
 
This incident was reported in the IACUC meeting minutes and corrective measures, including ensuring that vials are 
appropriately labeled with expiration dates, had been implemented prior to the inspection. 
3.80(a)(2)(iii) 
   
Primary enclosures. 
On January 8, 2026, a male cynomolgus macaque was found to have escaped from its primary enclosure. This animal 
sustained a minor hand injury and other minor wounds that were evaluated and treated by a veterinarian, which included 
suturing the hand wound as well as administering antibiotics and analgesics. Upon further investigation, the animal was 
discovered to have escaped by pushing a pan through a latch and exploiting the resulting gap that would contain a larger 
animal. 
 
Failure to house nonhuman primates in secure primary enclosures may allow them to escape and sustain injuries. The 
facility must ensure that primary enclosures are constructed and maintained to contain nonhuman primates securely and 
prevent accidental opening of the enclosure, including opening by the animal.  
 
This incident was reported in the IACUC meeting minutes and corrective measures, including locking the latch that the 
animal exploited, had been implemented prior to the inspection. To remain corrected from April 30, 2026, forward. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
